results.notes: Baseline authority resolution.

answer.title: Authority
answer.shortDefinition: Authority is recognized standing to direct, decide, or govern within a defined structure.
answer.coreMeaning: Authority functions by attaching directive right to a role, office, or position that others treat as operative. It depends on recognized standing rather than on force alone or on the validity of that standing.
answer.fullDefinition:
Authority is not any ability to produce effects. It is a structured right to issue direction, make decisions, or govern within an accepted role or office.

Its internal composition joins a directive claim to a recognized position. That position may be formal or informal, but the concept requires that the right to direct is treated as attached to the role rather than to mere capacity.

Within a system, authority organizes who may decide, command, or settle matters. Questions of whether that standing is justified belong to legitimacy, while questions of whether directives can be enforced belong to power.

sources.label: Oxford Languages
sources.usedFor: definition boundary for recognized right to direct or decide

sources.label: Max Weber, Economy and Society
sources.usedFor: structural distinction between recognized rule-bound command and mere power

results.notes: Baseline power resolution.

answer.title: Power
answer.shortDefinition: Power is effective capacity to shape actions, outcomes, or conditions with or without recognized standing.
answer.coreMeaning: Power functions through the ability to produce effects, secure compliance, or alter available conditions. It does not depend on acceptance, justification, or formal office.
answer.fullDefinition:
Power is not identical to authority or legitimacy. It names effective capacity rather than recognized right or accepted validity.

Its internal composition may rest in resources, coercive capacity, institutional position, coordination, or control over dependencies. The concept therefore concerns what can be made to happen, not whether that capacity is justified.

Within a system, power explains operative effect. Authority may channel it and legitimacy may justify it, but neither is required for power to exist or to work.

sources.label: Oxford Languages
sources.usedFor: definition boundary for capacity and ability to act or affect

sources.label: Steven Lukes, Power: A Radical View
sources.usedFor: structural grounding for power as effective capacity rather than formal right

results.notes: Baseline legitimacy resolution.

answer.title: Legitimacy
answer.shortDefinition: Legitimacy is accepted validity of a claim, rule, office, or standing within a defined order.
answer.coreMeaning: Legitimacy functions by marking an exercise of standing or rule as valid rather than merely operative. It concerns justification and acceptance, not the possession of force or the existence of a directive role by itself.
answer.fullDefinition:
Legitimacy is not the same as authority. Authority is the recognized right or standing to direct; legitimacy concerns whether that standing, rule, or claim counts as valid.

Its internal composition joins acceptance or justification to a rule, office, institution, or order. The concept therefore evaluates standing rather than conferring capacity.

Within a system, legitimacy stabilizes obedience, trust, and continuity by making power and authority count as valid. Power may operate without it, and authority may exist while its legitimacy is contested.

sources.label: Oxford Languages
sources.usedFor: definition boundary for accepted validity and proper standing

sources.label: David Beetham, The Legitimation of Power
sources.usedFor: structural distinction between valid standing and mere operative power

results.notes: Baseline responsibility resolution.

answer.title: Responsibility
answer.shortDefinition: Responsibility is answerable connection between an actor and attributable actions, roles, or outcomes.
answer.coreMeaning: Responsibility functions by linking conduct or effects back to an actor as something for which that actor can be called to account. It concerns answerability for what is attributable, not simply obedience to a required rule.
answer.fullDefinition:
Responsibility is not identical to duty. Duty concerns what must be done under a rule or role; responsibility concerns what an actor can be held answerable for.

Its internal structure joins attribution to answerability. The concept can attach to conduct, omission, stewardship, or resulting state, provided the actor is treated as bearing that connection.

Within a system, responsibility assigns who must answer for actions, decisions, or conditions. It therefore organizes attribution before judgment, sanction, or repair.

sources.label: Oxford Languages
sources.usedFor: definition boundary for answerability and assigned charge

sources.label: H. L. A. Hart, Punishment and Responsibility
sources.usedFor: structural grounding for attribution and answerability

results.notes: Baseline duty resolution.

answer.title: Duty
answer.shortDefinition: Duty is required conduct owed by an actor under a rule, role, or obligation.
answer.coreMeaning: Duty functions by attaching a required line of conduct to a position, office, or binding norm. It concerns what must be done or refrained from, not who is answerable for all resulting outcomes.
answer.fullDefinition:
Duty is not identical to responsibility. Responsibility links an actor to answerability; duty specifies conduct that is owed under a rule, role, office, or obligation.

Its internal structure joins requirement to an actor through a binding source. The concept therefore concerns directive obligation rather than mere preference or capacity.

Within a system, duty stabilizes expected conduct by marking what is required before action occurs. Whether failure produces broader responsibility is a further question, not the definition of duty itself.

sources.label: Oxford Languages
sources.usedFor: definition boundary for required conduct and obligation

sources.label: Wesley Newcomb Hohfeld, Fundamental Legal Conceptions
sources.usedFor: structural grounding for duty as owed conduct under a binding relation

answer.title: Duty
answer.shortDefinition: Duty is required conduct owed by an actor under a rule, role, or obligation.
answer.coreMeaning: Duty functions by attaching a required line of conduct to a position, office, or binding norm. It concerns what must be done or refrained from, not who is answerable for all resulting outcomes.
answer.fullDefinition:
Duty is not identical to responsibility. Responsibility links an actor to answerability; duty specifies conduct that is owed under a rule, role, office, or obligation.

Its internal structure joins requirement to an actor through a binding source. The concept therefore concerns directive obligation rather than mere preference or capacity.

Within a system, duty stabilizes expected conduct by marking what is required before action occurs. Whether failure produces broader responsibility is a further question, not the definition of duty itself.

sources.label: Oxford Languages
sources.usedFor: definition boundary for required conduct and obligation

sources.label: Wesley Newcomb Hohfeld, Fundamental Legal Conceptions
sources.usedFor: structural grounding for duty as owed conduct under a binding relation

answer.title: Authority
answer.shortDefinition: Authority is recognized standing to direct, decide, or govern within a defined structure.
answer.coreMeaning: Authority functions by attaching directive right to a role, office, or position that others treat as operative. It depends on recognized standing rather than on force alone or on the validity of that standing.
answer.fullDefinition:
Authority is not any ability to produce effects. It is a structured right to issue direction, make decisions, or govern within an accepted role or office.

Its internal composition joins a directive claim to a recognized position. That position may be formal or informal, but the concept requires that the right to direct is treated as attached to the role rather than to mere capacity.

Within a system, authority organizes who may decide, command, or settle matters. Questions of whether that standing is justified belong to legitimacy, while questions of whether directives can be enforced belong to power.

sources.label: Oxford Languages
sources.usedFor: definition boundary for recognized right to direct or decide

sources.label: Max Weber, Economy and Society
sources.usedFor: structural distinction between recognized rule-bound command and mere power

results.notes: Heavy punctuation around legitimacy should still resolve.

answer.title: Legitimacy
answer.shortDefinition: Legitimacy is accepted validity of a claim, rule, office, or standing within a defined order.
answer.coreMeaning: Legitimacy functions by marking an exercise of standing or rule as valid rather than merely operative. It concerns justification and acceptance, not the possession of force or the existence of a directive role by itself.
answer.fullDefinition:
Legitimacy is not the same as authority. Authority is the recognized right or standing to direct; legitimacy concerns whether that standing, rule, or claim counts as valid.

Its internal composition joins acceptance or justification to a rule, office, institution, or order. The concept therefore evaluates standing rather than conferring capacity.

Within a system, legitimacy stabilizes obedience, trust, and continuity by making power and authority count as valid. Power may operate without it, and authority may exist while its legitimacy is contested.

sources.label: Oxford Languages
sources.usedFor: definition boundary for accepted validity and proper standing

sources.label: David Beetham, The Legitimation of Power
sources.usedFor: structural distinction between valid standing and mere operative power

results.notes: Bracketed power should still resolve.

answer.title: Power
answer.shortDefinition: Power is effective capacity to shape actions, outcomes, or conditions with or without recognized standing.
answer.coreMeaning: Power functions through the ability to produce effects, secure compliance, or alter available conditions. It does not depend on acceptance, justification, or formal office.
answer.fullDefinition:
Power is not identical to authority or legitimacy. It names effective capacity rather than recognized right or accepted validity.

Its internal composition may rest in resources, coercive capacity, institutional position, coordination, or control over dependencies. The concept therefore concerns what can be made to happen, not whether that capacity is justified.

Within a system, power explains operative effect. Authority may channel it and legitimacy may justify it, but neither is required for power to exist or to work.

sources.label: Oxford Languages
sources.usedFor: definition boundary for capacity and ability to act or affect

sources.label: Steven Lukes, Power: A Radical View
sources.usedFor: structural grounding for power as effective capacity rather than formal right
